one_line_takeaway: AutoMalTool is a LangGraph multi-agent pipeline that automatically writes and iteratively refines poisoned MCP tool DESCRIPTIONS which preserve code function, evade MCP-Scan/A.I.G at ~77-98% rates, and manipulate real agents (Claude Desktop, Cline) at ~35% average effective success for about $0.03 per tool.
key_finding: AutoMalTool reliably produces poisoned MCP tools that both work and evade detection, at trivial cost. Generation succeeds at high rates (GSR 93.1% Alpaca, 89.7% WhatsApp, 72.3% Filesystem; ~85% average). On real agents the average ESR is ~35.3% (exceeding 70% in the best cases), so a meaningful minority of generated tools actually manipulate deployed agents. IPI attacks are consistently more effective than ORM (ORM is harder because it must both complete the task and convincingly alter output). Model/agent robustness varies: Claude Opus 4.1 is the most robust backend across all settings; Cline is somewhat more robust than Claude Desktop; and, counterintuitively, GPT-4.1 resisted better than the newer GPT-5-chat-latest, indicating newer/more-capable models are not automatically more resistant. The tools are highly evasive — evasion rates run roughly 77-98% (the abstract frames this as detection rates of only ~11.1% for MCP-Scan and ~23.4% for A.I.G). ORM evades detection better than IPI. Injecting special Unicode tokens (e.g., bullet, arrow, warning glyphs) into descriptions boosts attack effectiveness. Cost is ~$0.03 and <15,000 tokens per tool, ~200s and ~2 optimization iterations on average.
core_contribution: The paper presents AutoMalTool, which the authors position as the first automated red-teaming framework that generates MCP (Model Context Protocol) server packages containing malicious MCP tools to attack LLM-based agents via tool-poisoning. Its own novelty is a multi-agent generation pipeline (built on LangGraph) with four collaborating components: (1) an Initial Generator that infers plausible user tasks for a benign tool and drafts a poisoned tool DESCRIPTION targeting one of two attacker goals; (2) an Oracle that acts as an LLM-based prompt-injection detector to check evasiveness; (3) an Effectiveness Evaluator that runs a simulated agent (two instances of one LLM, one with the benign tool and one with the poisoned tool) plus an LLM behavior-judger to confirm the malicious behavior actually manifests; and (4) a Tool Optimizer that iteratively rephrases the description to improve either evasiveness or effectiveness, using a 'false-positive'/'legitimate functionality' framing to bypass the optimizing LLM's own safety alignment. Crucially, the framework only edits tool METADATA (name, description, input schema) and uses a static-code-analysis modifier to splice the poisoned description back into the package, so the underlying code stays syntactically valid and functional. This is distinct from the cited Related Work (AgentDojo, RedCode, AgentVigil, and prior MCP position papers by Fang et al. and Song et al.), which the authors characterize as either direct-injection red-teaming that assumes the attacker talks to the agent directly, or manual proof-of-concept MCP demos (e.g., Song et al. gave only three weather-related examples).
threat_model: Black-box supply-chain attack. The adversary is a malicious user who crafts MCP server packages containing poisoned tools and uploads them to open-source package repositories (PyPI, npm) or MCP marketplaces (MCP.so, Smithery); the paper notes these repositories lack integrity/verification mechanisms. An agent developer who unwittingly installs the package gets its agent's behavior altered when the tool is used for specific user tasks. The attacker has NO knowledge of the target agent's internals (system architecture, backend LLM) and must assume defenders run detection tools, so generated tools must evade detection while preserving code functionality. The paper scopes attacker objectives to exactly two malicious behaviors: (a) Incorrect Parameter Invocation (IPI), corrupting the parameter-grounding stage (e.g., altering a stock-trade tool's parameters), and (b) Output Results Misinterpretation/Manipulation (ORM), corrupting the result-interpretation stage (e.g., making an agent draw wrong conclusions from market data). Explicitly out of scope: tool-selection attacks, data exfiltration, and tool-shadowing.
limitations: By the authors' own account: only two malicious-behavior classes are covered (no exfiltration, tool-shadowing, or unauthorized-system-access); they call for future work on these. Reviewer-noted caveats grounded in the paper: (1) The headline GSR (~85%) is measured in a SIMULATED agent, and the real-agent ESR is only ~35.3%, so most 'successfully generated' tools do NOT actually manipulate a deployed agent — the two numbers should not be conflated. (2) Both success and evasion depend heavily on LLM-as-judge components (the behavior judger and the Oracle), whose reliability is not independently validated. (3) The A.I.G detector was a Python reimplementation of only its core module, not the shipped tool, and both detectors are LLM/heuristic prompt-injection scanners rather than runtime behavioral monitors, so evasion rates may not transfer to defenses that observe execution. (4) Evaluation covers just 2 agents and 3 MCP servers; ESR is reported on random samples with non-completable tasks excluded. (5) The framework only poisons metadata/descriptions, so defenses that ignore natural-language description text would be unaffected. Ethically, the authors withhold public release, sharing code only with verified researchers and not uploading malicious packages to public repositories.

datasets_used: Alpaca MCP Server (financial trading, 27 tools, 135 user tasks, 247 generation tasks)
WhatsApp MCP Server (messaging, 12 tools, 60 user tasks, 117 generation tasks; 4,800+ GitHub stars)
Filesystem MCP Server (file operations, 14 tools, 70 user tasks, 130 generation tasks; 1.6M+ npm downloads)
53 MCP tools total across the three servers
Target agents: Claude Desktop and Cline
Backend/component LLMs: DeepSeek V3.1, Claude Sonnet 4, Claude Opus 4.1, Claude Opus 4, Claude Sonnet 3.7, GPT-5-chat-latest, GPT-4.1
quantitative_results: GSR (generation success, simulated): Alpaca 93.1%, WhatsApp 89.7%, Filesystem 72.3% (~85% average)
ESR (effective success on real agents): ~35.3% average, over 70% in best cases

ESR examples: Claude Desktop + Claude Opus 4 IPI on Alpaca = 71.4%; Claude Desktop + Claude Sonnet 4 IPI on Alpaca = 71.4% vs Cline + Claude Sonnet 4 IPI on Alpaca = 57.1%; Cline + DeepSeek V3.1 IPI on WhatsApp = 83.3%; Cline + GPT-5-chat-latest IPI on Alpaca = 77.8%
Claude Opus 4.1 most robust backend overall; GPT-4.1 more robust than GPT-5-chat-latest
IPI attacks outperform ORM attacks across settings
Evasion rate ER vs MCP-Scan: IPI 88.9%/86.7%/87.8%, ORM 95.5%/97.5%/92.2% (Alpaca/WhatsApp/Filesystem)
Overheads: ~$0.03 and <15,000 tokens per tool; ~200s generation time; ~2 optimization iterations (max N=10)
MCP adoption stats cited from MCP docs: 97.4% of listed agents support tools, 34.6% support resources, 30.1% support prompts
baselines_compared: MCP-Scan (Invariant Labs) - SOTA static metadata detector, evaded
A.I.G / AI-Infra-Guard (Tencent) - SOTA LLM-agent code detector, core module reimplemented and evaded
Prior MCP tool-poisoning work referenced as manual/PoC only: Song et al. (three weather examples), Fang et al.
Related red-teaming frameworks contrasted (not run as baselines): AgentDojo, RedCode, AgentVigil
